title: The Rosicrucian manifestos
blurb: The anonymous Fama and Confessio announced a hidden brotherhood of Christian alchemist-sages, sparking a Europe-wide furore and a new myth of the invisible college.
source: Tobias Churton, The Golden Builders (alchemy corpus)

title: Maier's Atalanta Fugiens
blurb: Michael Maier's masterpiece pairs fifty alchemical emblems with epigrams and fugues for three voices — an alchemical book you look at, read, and hear at once.
mtg: Ted's own Atalanta projects grow from this book; alchemy as multimedia emblem.
source: Ted's alchemy DBs; Peter Forshaw (alchemy corpus)

title: Kepler, where astrology and astronomy still touch
blurb: Even as he found the laws of planetary motion, Kepler cast horoscopes for a living and sought a 'harmony of the world' — the two star-sciences not yet divorced.

title: Robert Fludd's cosmic engravings
blurb: Fludd's vast illustrated works mapped a Hermetic universe of macrocosm and microcosm, defending magic against the new mechanical philosophy.

title: Ashmole's Theatrum Chemicum Britannicum
blurb: Elias Ashmole gathered England's alchemical poetry into one volume, presenting the art as a venerable native tradition just as its scientific credibility was ebbing.
source: Stanton Linden, The Alchemy Reader (alchemy corpus)

title: Boyle's The Sceptical Chymist
blurb: Robert Boyle attacked the old element-theories and pushed toward a corpuscular chemistry — yet he too believed in transmutation and lobbied to repeal the ban on it.
mtg: The hinge where alchemy begins to become chemistry.

title: Newton's alchemical decades
blurb: Isaac Newton wrote more on alchemy than on physics, hunting the philosophers' mercury and the principles of transmutation in secret for thirty years.
source: Principe, The Secrets of Alchemy; Newman (alchemy corpus)

title: The Lemegeton (Lesser Key of Solomon) takes shape
blurb: Assembled from older material, the Lemegeton's first book, the Ars Goetia, catalogues seventy-two demons with their seals and offices — the master-index of infernal magic.
mtg: The direct ancestor of every 'bind a named demon' card.
source: Joseph Peterson (ed.), The Lesser Key of Solomon

title: The Key of Solomon proliferates
blurb: Manuscript copies of the Clavicula Salomonis spread the Solomonic pentacles — discs of divine names and characters for every planetary operation.
source: Joseph Peterson (ed.), Key of Solomon; RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. pentaculum

title: The Salem witch trials
blurb: Twenty people were executed on spectral evidence in colonial Massachusetts — the most famous, and nearly the last, great outbreak of the witch panic.
source: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic (context)

title: The decline of magic
blurb: Keith Thomas's great thesis: as insurance, medicine, and the mechanical philosophy advanced, educated Europe quietly stopped believing that magic worked — without ever quite disproving it.
mtg: The moment magic became fantasy — the precondition for a game about it.
source: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic (renaissance magic corpus)

title: Cheap-print grimoires: the Grimorium Verum
blurb: Grimoires like the Grimorium Verum and Grand Grimoire moved into cheap print, feeding a popular market for spells long after the learned tradition had gone respectable.

title: Britain stops prosecuting witchcraft
blurb: The Witchcraft Act of 1736 repealed the death penalty for witchcraft, punishing instead the pretence of magical powers — the law now disbelieved in the crime.
source: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic

title: Court de Gébelin makes the Tarot 'Egyptian'
blurb: Antoine Court de Gébelin declared the Tarot a lost Egyptian book of wisdom, launching the cards' second life as instruments of divination.
source: Michael Dummett, The Game of Tarot (context)

title: Mesmer and animal magnetism
blurb: Franz Mesmer's 'animal magnetism' produced trance and 'clairvoyant' subjects — a secular occultism that seeded Spiritualism and the word clairvoyance itself.
